title: AdS3 Quantum Gravity via Finite-N Expansions
speaker: Wei Li
abstract: I will discuss the bulk mechanism of the stringy exclusion principle in the AdS3/CFT2 case. Consider the duality between  string theory on AdS3 × S3 × M4 and Sym^N(M4). At finite N, both the chiral-chiral spectrum and the number of the polar terms of the elliptic genus are finite. I will show that these finite-N spectra can be recovered by summing over the corresponding contribution from the  (AdS3xS3)/Z_k x M4 orbifolds. Although each orbifold contributes an infinite series, their sum reproduces the finite-N boundary data due to the large cancellations induced by the bosonic negative modes of the orbifold saddles.

proof_stages.label: Finite-N residue expansion and prototype exclusion mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the finite-N AdS/CFT problem and the stringy exclusion principle, introduces a contour-residue finite-N expansion of the grand canonical partition function, and calibrates the mechanism in the half-BPS AdS5/N=4 case where analytic continuation of negative modes gives alternating-sign cancellations.

proof_stages.label: AdS3/CFT2 protected-sector target data
proof_stages.summary: For the D1-D5 AdS3 x S3 x M4 system with symmetric-product boundary CFT, the half-BPS grand canonical function obtained from the DMVV formula supplies exact finite-N coefficients and identifies the light, medium, and heavy regimes to be reproduced from the bulk.

proof_stages.label: Pole families, Z_k images, and Stokes chambers
proof_stages.summary: The grand canonical function is analyzed to reveal four Hodge-diamond pole families, their Z_k images accumulating toward walls of singularities, and the chamber dependence that determines how residue expansions must be chosen.

proof_stages.label: Negative-mode criterion and exact finite-N reconstruction
proof_stages.summary: The residue prescription keeps precisely the poles inside the singularity wall, whose chamber-dependent expansions have finitely many negative modes and hence finite-energy contributions, while outside poles have infinitely many negative modes and do not contribute, so the alternating residue sum reconstructs the finite-N spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Orbifold-saddle bulk interpretation
proof_stages.summary: The contributing residue factors are identified with excitations around backreacted Z_k orbifold geometries of AdS3 x S3, with fractional spectral flow in the worldsheet description and a worldsheet computation reproducing the residue partition functions.

proof_stages.label: Quarter-BPS polar-term extension
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reports an in-progress extension to quarter-BPS elliptic-genus polar terms below the black-hole threshold, where the same finite-N/orbifold matching appears after allowing more general orbifolds.

proof_stages.label: Synthesis, regime comparison, and outlook
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion compares the AdS5 and AdS3 meanings of light, medium, and heavy states, summarizes the role of orbifold saddles and negative-mode energy upshifts in enforcing finite-N constraints, and lists direct bulk computations and boundary trace-relation analogues as future goals.

examples.name: k=1 and k=2 signs in the AdS5 half-BPS trace-relation expansion
examples.note: The speaker uses the first two residue sectors to show concretely that a minus sign counts a trace relation while the next plus sign counts relations among relations.
examples.engagement_reason: He expands the AdS5 half-BPS residue terms and interprets the k=1 minus sign as a trace relation and the k=2 plus sign as a relation among relations.

passes.reason: The k=1 and k=2 signs are ordinary early terms in a systematic alternating finite-N trace-relation expansion, used explanatorily rather than adversarially.

examples.name: Diagonal chamber y = ybar residue-sum demonstration
examples.note: A special chamber where only one family of poles contributes is used to display how increasing the cutoff in the k-sum successively matches more finite-N coefficients and pushes the unmatched tail away.
examples.engagement_reason: He sets y=ybar and explicitly demonstrates the residue-sum with increasing k cutoffs, explaining how the coefficients successively match the finite-N target.

passes.reason: The specialization y = ybar collapses two fugacities to a left-right symmetric diagonal slice for the residue-sum check.
the specialization y = ybar collapses the two-fugacity setting to an exchange-symmetric diagonal slice for the residue check
Setting y=ybar is a left-right symmetric diagonal specialization that collapses the two-fugacity chamber to a lower-dimensional slice for a residue-sum demonstration.

the diagonal y=\bar y slice is left-right symmetric and collapses the two fugacities for a residue-sum check

examples.name: k=3 fractional spectral flow and cycle splitting
examples.note: The k=3 orbifold case illustrates how fractional spectral flow permits a three-cycle state to break into smaller cycles, producing modes with negative energy relative to the new vacuum.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the k=3 case by using fractional spectral flow 1/3 to explain how a three-cycle can split into smaller cycles and produce negative energy relative to the new vacuum.

passes.reason: The k=3 case is systematic, but the highlighted splitting of a 3-cycle into shorter cycles is a reducible degeneration of the long-cycle sector.
the k=3 fractional spectral-flow and cycle-splitting case is an ordinary representative of the cyclic-orbifold family

examples.name: Boundary new-vacuum picture in Q&A
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains the orbifold boundary interpretation as the same symmetric-product CFT with the vacuum changed from many one-cycles to many k-cycles, clarifying the origin of negative modes.
examples.engagement_reason: In the Q&A he describes the boundary as the same symmetric orbifold with a new k-cycle vacuum, but only states this interpretation without working through it.

passes.reason: The boundary new vacuum is a no-excitation ground state with uniform k-cycle structure, hence minimal and highly regular rather than a counterexample.
the all-k-cycle new vacuum is an unexcited ground/base state and a uniform regular cycle configuration
